meta.title: MomOps Interdependency Graph
meta.description: Structured dataset showing how care-system disruptions cascade across MomOps evaluation dimensions, infrastructure, actors, and outcomes.

nodes.label: Childcare cancellation
nodes.description: Planned childcare becomes unavailable at short notice

nodes.label: Sick child at home
nodes.description: Unexpected illness creates additional care load and schedule instability

nodes.label: Washing machine failure
nodes.description: Domestic infrastructure failure increases hidden labor over multiple days

nodes.label: Return to work after maternity leave
nodes.description: Work re-entry occurs during an unresolved care transition

nodes.label: Specialist appointment conflict
nodes.description: Important care appointment overlaps with school work and transport constraints

nodes.label: Childcare availability
nodes.description: Access to stable external care support

nodes.label: Transport coordination
nodes.description: Reliable movement between home school work and care sites

nodes.label: School schedule
nodes.description: Pickup drop-off and attendance timing constraints

nodes.label: Employer flexibility
nodes.description: Capacity of paid work systems to absorb care disruptions

nodes.label: Domestic appliance access
nodes.description: Availability of household tools that reduce repeated manual labor

nodes.label: Support network
nodes.description: Presence of family friends or community backup

nodes.label: Interruption resilience
nodes.description: Ability to preserve the primary care objective under disruption

nodes.label: Care infrastructure awareness
nodes.description: Recognition of the systems and logistics that make care possible

nodes.label: Care debt detection
nodes.description: Recognition of burdens deferred into the future

nodes.label: Non-linear journey handling
nodes.description: Ability to reason across fragmented transitions and handoffs

nodes.label: Surveillance risk
nodes.description: Need to avoid invasive or burdensome monitoring responses

nodes.label: Reciprocity balance
nodes.description: Whether adaptation is distributed rather than defaulted onto caregivers

nodes.label: Mother
nodes.description: Primary caregiver managing care and administrative coordination

nodes.label: Child
nodes.description: Dependent receiving care and affected by timing or continuity failures

nodes.label: Employer
nodes.description: Institution imposing or relaxing work constraints

nodes.label: Childcare provider
nodes.description: Formal or informal provider sustaining external care capacity

nodes.label: Late arrival to work
nodes.description: Paid work lateness caused by care-system disruption

nodes.label: Missed appointment
nodes.description: Essential healthcare or care coordination appointment is lost

nodes.label: Accumulated care debt
nodes.description: Deferred tasks and strain compound over subsequent days

nodes.label: Maternal overload
nodes.description: Cognitive emotional and logistical strain concentrates on the mother

nodes.label: Reduced household stability
nodes.description: Family routine becomes less predictable and more fragile

links.explanation: Childcare cancellation directly reduces available childcare support
Loss of childcare immediately tests interruption resilience
Re-routing transport and pickup plans becomes necessary
School timing becomes harder to meet when childcare collapses
Work systems must absorb sudden care instability
The burden often falls back onto the primary caregiver
Childcare breakdown increases maternal overload
Tasks displaced by cancellation build up into care debt
Sick child conditions create repeated interruptions
Illness makes support infrastructure and contingencies newly visible
Sickness intensifies caregiving strain and vigilance
Routine tasks are deferred while illness is prioritized
Loss of washing capacity removes key domestic infrastructure
Infrastructure dependence becomes central to planning
Manual workarounds accumulate over multiple days
Appliance failure creates compounding hidden labor
Household stability decreases as laundry and hygiene routines fragment
Return to work after leave is a transition not a reset
Employer flexibility strongly shapes re-entry feasibility
Inflexible onboarding shifts adaptation burden to the mother
Unacknowledged re-entry strain increases overload
Appointment conflicts require non-linear coordination
Transport timing affects whether the appointment is kept
School schedule constrains feasible appointment planning
Poor coordination can lead to a missed appointment
Stable childcare is a visible part of care infrastructure
Transport coordination enables cross-context transitions
School schedules shape resilience under interruption
Employer flexibility affects whether burden is shared or individualized
Household tools reduce long-tail deferred labor
Backup support improves resilience under disruption
Low interruption resilience can produce lateness to work
Ignoring infrastructure increases fragility in household routine
Failure to detect care debt allows burden to accumulate
Poor handling of transitions increases missed appointment risk
Low reciprocity concentrates adaptation burden onto the mother
The mother is the actor most likely to absorb overload
The child is directly affected by lost appointments or schedule failures
Employer policy influences available flexibility
Childcare providers sustain external care capacity

example_failure_paths.label: Childcare collapse → maternal overload
example_failure_paths.description: Childcare cancellation removes infrastructure support, stressing interruption resilience and exposing reciprocity imbalance, leading to maternal overload.

example_failure_paths.label: Washing machine failure → accumulated care debt
example_failure_paths.description: Domestic appliance failure removes a key infrastructure node, increasing care debt that compounds over subsequent days.

example_failure_paths.label: Return to work → non-linear transition strain
example_failure_paths.description: Return to work after maternity leave stresses non-linear journey handling; if employer flexibility is low, reciprocity imbalance concentrates strain on the mother.

example_failure_paths.label: Specialist appointment conflict → missed appointment
example_failure_paths.description: Appointment timing conflicts with transport and school constraints, stressing non-linear coordination and risking a missed appointment.
